Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing DOWN 3 times:
- Black object: rows 27-35 (was 18-26, moved +9)
- Charcoal object: rows 27-35 (was 18-26, moved +9)
- Yellow shape: rows 45-53 (unchanged)
- Yellow bar: 52 pixels (was 56, decreased by 4... wait, that's 3 DOWN actions + 1 check action = 4 actions, but the bar decreased by 4, so each action decreases the bar by 1)
Wait, the charcoal object also moved DOWN! It was at rows 18-26 and now it's at rows 27-35. So pressing DOWN moves both the black and charcoal objects DOWN by 3 rows each.
So the movement mechanics are:
- RIGHT: black moves +3 cols, charcoal moves -3 cols (they move horizontally in opposite directions)
- DOWN: black moves +3 rows, charcoal moves +3 rows (they move vertically in the same direction)
- LEFT: probably black moves -3 cols, charcoal moves +3 cols (opposite of RIGHT)
- UP: probably black moves -3 rows, charcoal moves -3 rows (opposite of DOWN)
Now I understand the mechanics better. The black and charcoal objects move together vertically but in opposite directions horizontally.
The goal might be to position the black object so that it overlaps with the yellow T-shape. The yellow shape is at rows 45-53, cols 51-59. The black object is at rows 27-35, cols 39-47.
To overlap:
- Row difference: 45 - 27 = 18 rows. Need 6 more DOWNs (18/3).
- Col difference: 51 - 39 = 12 cols. Need 4 more RIGHTs (12/3).
But wait, when I press RIGHT, the charcoal object moves left. This might cause the charcoal object to go off-screen or interact with something. Let me check the current positions:
- Black object: rows 27-35, cols 39-47
- Charcoal object: rows 27-35, cols 15-23
- Yellow shape: rows 45-53, cols 51-59
If I press RIGHT 4 times:
- Black object: cols 39+12=51 to 47+12=59 (matches yellow shape cols!)
- Charcoal object: cols 15-12=3 to 23-12=11 (still on screen)
If I press DOWN 6 times:
- Black object: rows 27+18=45 to 35+18=53 (matches yellow shape rows!)
- Charcoal object: rows 27+18=45 to 35+18=53 (also at yellow shape rows!)
So if I press RIGHT 4 times and DOWN 6 times, the black object will overlap with the yellow shape, and the charcoal object will also be at the same rows as the yellow shape.
But wait, the order matters. If I press RIGHT first, the charcoal object moves left. If I press DOWN first, both objects move down. Let me think about the order.
Actually, since the movements are independent (horizontal and vertical), the order shouldn't matter for the final position. Let me just move the black object to overlap with the yellow shape.
But I'm still not sure what happens when the black object overlaps with the yellow shape. Maybe it transforms the yellow shape, or maybe it completes the level.
Let me try moving the black object to the yellow shape's position. I'll batch the moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move black object to overlap with yellow shape
# Current black: rows 27-35, cols 39-47
# Target yellow: rows 45-53, cols 51-59
# Need: +18 rows (6 DOWNs), +12 cols (4 RIGHTs)

# Let me batch the moves
moves = ['DOWN']*6 + ['RIGHT']*4
result = action(moves)
print(f"Result: {result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter moves:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 40:
        print(f"  Black object: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        print(f"  Charcoal object: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"  Yellow shape: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] > 40:
        print(f"  Yellow bar: pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check if level completed
if result.get('level_completed') or result.get('done') or result.get('game_over'):
    print("\n*** LEVEL COMPLETED OR GAME OVER ***")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
